title: The Blood of the Covenant
summary: The old covenant was sealed with the blood of animals. The new covenant was sealed with something far more precious — the blood of Christ.
bodyText:
When Moses sealed the covenant at Sinai, he took the blood of animals and sprinkled it on the people, saying: "This is the blood of the covenant that the Lord has made with you."

Blood sealed the deal. That was always how covenants worked in the ancient world. A covenant without blood was not binding.

Centuries later, in an upper room, Jesus took a cup and said something that would have stopped every Jewish heart in the room:

"This is My blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins." — Matthew 26:28

My blood. Not the blood of bulls and goats. My blood.

Jesus was announcing a new covenant — sealed not with an animal sacrifice, but with His own life.

The writer of Hebrews explains the superiority of this sacrifice:

"How much more, then, will the blood of Christ, who through the eternal Spirit offered Himself unblemished to God, cleanse our consciences from acts that lead to death, so that we may serve the living God!" — Hebrews 9:14

The old blood covered. The new blood cleanses.
The old blood had to be offered repeatedly. The new blood was offered once.
The old blood pointed forward. The new blood fulfills.

Every time we take this cup, we are participating in a covenant that was purchased at the highest price imaginable. We are not just sipping juice. We are remembering the blood that ratified our relationship with God.

This bread — a body given to seal the covenant.
This cup — the blood of the new covenant, poured out for you.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Spotless Lamb
summary: The sacrificial system required lambs without blemish. Jesus was the only truly spotless Lamb — and His perfection is what makes our redemption secure.
suggestedHymns: Alas, and Did My Savior Bleed
bodyText:
Under the old covenant, when someone brought a lamb for sacrifice, the animal had to be inspected. No broken bones. No blemishes. No defects. It had to be without spot.

That requirement was not arbitrary. It taught Israel — and it teaches us — that what is offered to God must be perfect. Nothing flawed can atone for sin.

And every lamb offered on every altar was, at best, a shadow. They were physically spotless, but they were still animals. They could not truly understand what they were dying for. And their blood could never permanently remove sin.

But Peter writes about a different Lamb:

"You were redeemed… with the precious blood of Christ, a lamb without blemish or defect." — 1 Peter 1:18–19

Jesus was not just physically spotless. He was morally spotless. Spiritually spotless. In every thought, every motive, every word, every action — He was without sin.

Hebrews 9:14 says He "offered Himself unblemished to God." The perfection of the sacrifice is what gives it its power.

This is why our salvation is secure. It does not rest on a flawed offering. It does not depend on a sacrifice that might have been insufficient. It rests on the only truly spotless Lamb who ever lived — and who willingly laid down His life.

If the lamb had been blemished, the offering would have been rejected. But Jesus was perfect. And His offering was accepted — fully, finally, forever.

This bread — the body of the spotless Lamb.
This cup — the precious blood, without blemish or defect.

Our redemption is secure because the Lamb was perfect.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Made Alive Together
summary: We were dead in our sins. But God, being rich in mercy, made us alive together with Christ. The Lord's Supper celebrates the miracle of being raised from spiritual death.
bodyText:
Paul does not begin Ephesians 2 with a compliment. He begins with a diagnosis:

"You were dead in your transgressions and sins."

Dead. Not sick. Not struggling. Not underperforming. Dead.

That is the condition of every person apart from Christ. Spiritually lifeless. Unable to revive themselves. Unable to earn their way back to God.

But then come four of the most beautiful words in Scripture:

"But because of His great love for us, God, who is rich in mercy, made us alive with Christ even when we were dead in transgressions — it is by grace you have been saved." — Ephesians 2:4–5

But God.

Those two words change everything. We were dead — but God. We were helpless — but God. We had no hope — but God, who is rich in mercy.

And what did He do? He "made us alive together with Christ." Not independently. Not on our own. Together with Him. Our life is bound to His life. His resurrection is the engine of our new birth.

Paul goes even further in verse 6: "And God raised us up with Christ and seated us with Him in the heavenly realms in Christ Jesus."

We are not just alive. We are raised. We are seated. In God's eyes, our position is already secure — because it rests not on our performance, but on Christ's resurrection.

That is what this table celebrates. Not just the death of Jesus — but the life that death produced.

This bread — a body that died so we could be made alive.
This cup — blood shed so the dead could be raised.

We were dead. But God made us alive. And that changes everything.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Power of the Blood
summary: How can a holy God justify sinners and remain just? The answer is the blood of Christ — the only sacrifice that satisfies both justice and mercy.
bodyText:
There is a question at the heart of the gospel that most people never stop to ask: How can a holy God forgive sinners and still be just?

If God simply overlooked sin, He would not be just. If He only punished sin, there would be no hope. Somehow, justice and mercy had to meet. Somehow, sin had to be addressed and sinners had to be saved.

Paul answers this in Romans 3:24–26:

"All are justified freely by His grace through the redemption that came by Christ Jesus. God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement, through the shedding of His blood — to be received by faith. He did this to demonstrate His righteousness… so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus."

There it is. The cross is where justice and mercy met. At Calvary, God poured out the full penalty for sin — not on us, but on Christ. Justice was served. The debt was paid. And mercy was extended — freely, to everyone who puts their faith in Jesus.

The blood of Christ is not just a symbol. It is the mechanism by which God remains righteous while declaring sinners righteous. Without it, we have no standing before God. With it, we are justified — declared not guilty — by the Judge of all the earth.

That is the power of the blood. It does not merely soothe our conscience. It satisfies God's justice.

This bread — a body that absorbed the penalty we deserved.
This cup — blood that made it possible for a holy God to welcome unholy people.

At this table, justice and mercy embrace. And we are the beneficiaries.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Weight of the Cross
summary: Simon of Cyrene carried the physical cross. But Jesus carried something far heavier — the sin of the entire world.
bodyText:
Luke 23:26 records a detail that is easy to read past:

"As the soldiers led Him away, they seized Simon from Cyrene, who was on his way in from the country, and put the cross on him and made him carry it behind Jesus."

Jesus had been scourged. His back was torn. His body was failing. And the weight of the crossbeam was more than He could physically bear in that moment. So the soldiers grabbed a stranger from the crowd and forced him to carry it.

Simon carried the wood. But Jesus carried something heavier.

Isaiah 53:6 says:

"We all, like sheep, have gone astray, each of us has turned to our own way; and the Lord has laid on Him the iniquity of us all."

The iniquity of us all. Every sin of every person who would ever live — past, present, and future — was placed on Jesus.

The cross was heavy. But the sin was heavier. The wood could be shared. The guilt could not.

No one could help Him carry that. No human could bear even a fraction of it. The Son of God alone had the strength — and the willingness — to take it all.

So when we take the bread this morning, we are not remembering a man who was simply exhausted. We are remembering a Savior who was crushed under the weight of our rebellion — willingly, lovingly, completely.

When we take the cup, we are remembering that the blood He shed was the cost of removing that weight from us and placing it on Himself.

The cross was heavy. But His love was stronger.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: A Better Covenant
summary: The old covenant pointed forward. The new covenant, sealed by the blood of Jesus, is better in every way — better promises, a better mediator, and a better sacrifice.
suggestedHymns: It Is Well with My Soul
bodyText:
The writer of Hebrews makes a bold claim:

"But in fact the ministry Jesus has received is as superior to theirs as the covenant of which He is mediator is superior to the old one, since the new covenant is established on better promises." — Hebrews 8:6

Better promises. That is what the new covenant offers — not just a fresh version of the old system, but something fundamentally superior.

Jeremiah saw it coming. Centuries before Jesus, God spoke through him:

"The days are coming, declares the Lord, when I will make a new covenant… I will put My law in their minds and write it on their hearts. I will be their God, and they will be My people… For I will forgive their wickedness and will remember their sins no more." — Jeremiah 31:31–34

Under the old covenant, the law was written on stone. Under the new, it is written on hearts.
Under the old, sacrifices had to be repeated. Under the new, one sacrifice is sufficient.
Under the old, sins were remembered year after year. Under the new, God says, "I will remember their sins no more."

And the mediator of this new covenant is not Moses. It is Jesus — the One who not only delivered the terms but paid the price.

Every time we take the cup, we are participating in that new covenant. Jesus Himself said, "This cup is the new covenant in My blood." His blood is the ink that signed it. His death is the seal that ratified it.

This bread — the body of our covenant mediator.
This cup — the blood that established better promises.

We are not living under the shadow anymore. We are living in the reality. And that reality is built on the finished work of Christ.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Delivered from Darkness
summary: We have been rescued from the domain of darkness and brought into the kingdom of God's Son. The cross was not just forgiveness — it was a transfer of kingdoms.
bodyText:
Paul writes in Colossians 1:13–14:

"For He has rescued us from the dominion of darkness and brought us into the kingdom of the Son He loves, in whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins."

Notice the language. This is not a gentle invitation to change neighborhoods. This is a rescue operation.

"Rescued" implies danger. It implies captivity. It implies that we were in a place we could not escape on our own.

And that place is called "the dominion of darkness." Not just the presence of darkness — the dominion. The authority. The rule. We were not just visiting darkness. We were under its power.

Sin is not a minor inconvenience. It is a kingdom — and before Christ, we were citizens of it, whether we realized it or not. It shaped our thinking, directed our desires, and determined our destiny.

But God did not leave us there.

He rescued us. He transferred us. He moved us from one kingdom to another — from darkness into the kingdom of His beloved Son.

And the means of that transfer? "In whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins." The blood of Christ is our passport. The cross is the border crossing. And on this side, there is light, life, and belonging.

So when we gather at this table, we are not just remembering a death. We are celebrating a rescue. We are not just acknowledging our sin. We are proclaiming our deliverance.

This bread — the body that broke the chains of darkness.
This cup — the blood that bought our citizenship in the kingdom of light.

We have been delivered. And we are never going back.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Altar and the Table
summary: The writer of Hebrews says we have an altar — not of stone, but of sacrifice. The Lord's table connects us to the cross where Jesus suffered outside the gate.
suggestedHymns: When I Survey the Wondrous Cross
bodyText:
The writer of Hebrews makes a striking statement:

"We have an altar from which those who minister at the tabernacle have no right to eat." — Hebrews 13:10

An altar. Not a physical altar made of stone or bronze. But an altar nonetheless — a place of sacrifice. And that altar is the cross.

Under the old system, the high priest carried the blood of animals into the Most Holy Place. But the bodies of those sacrificed animals were burned outside the camp. They were taken beyond the boundaries of the community.

And then comes verse 12:

"And so Jesus also suffered outside the city gate to make the people holy through His own blood."

Jesus was not crucified in the temple. He was not sacrificed on a golden altar. He suffered outside the gate — in a place of rejection, shame, and disgrace.

That detail matters. It tells us that Jesus entered our exile. He went to the place of cursing. He was cast out — not because He deserved it, but because we did. He bore our shame in the most public, most humiliating way possible.

And from that altar — that rough, bloody, despised cross — comes our holiness. His blood, shed outside the gate, is what makes us clean.

So when we gather at this table, we are gathering around that altar. Not a sanitized version of it. Not a comfortable one. The real one — where the Son of God suffered and bled for our redemption.

This bread — a body broken outside the gate.
This cup — blood shed at the altar of the cross.

We have an altar. And it has changed everything.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: Remember and Proclaim
summary: The Lord's Supper is not passive — it is an act of proclamation. Every time we eat and drink, we are preaching the gospel without saying a word.
suggestedHymns: Tell Me the Story of Jesus
I Love to Tell the Story
bodyText:
Jesus said two words that define this meal: remember and do.

"Do this in remembrance of Me." — 1 Corinthians 11:24

But Paul adds something that deepens the act beyond personal reflection:

"For whenever you eat this bread and drink this cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until He comes." — 1 Corinthians 11:26

Proclaim. That is not a passive word. It means to declare, to announce, to preach.

Every time we gather around this table, we are doing more than remembering privately. We are preaching publicly. Without a pulpit. Without a microphone. Without a single word spoken aloud.

The bread and the cup are a sermon.

They say to one another: "His body was given. His blood was shed. And we believe it matters."
They say to the watching world: "We are people who have staked our lives on a crucified and risen Lord."
They say to heaven: "We have not forgotten."

This is one reason the early church gathered every first day of the week to break bread. It was not routine. It was not ritual. It was proclamation. It was the church, week after week, standing together and declaring: "Jesus died. Jesus rose. Jesus is coming again."

So this morning, as you take the bread and the cup, know that you are not just receiving — you are proclaiming. You are joining a declaration that stretches back two thousand years and forward to the day He returns.

This bread — a proclamation of a body given.
This cup — a proclamation of a covenant sealed in blood.

Let the table preach.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.

title: The Mediator
summary: There is one mediator between God and man — the man Christ Jesus. At the Lord's table, we remember the only One who could stand between us and a holy God.
suggestedHymns: What a Friend We Have in Jesus
bodyText:
Paul writes in 1 Timothy 2:5–6:

"For there is one God and one mediator between God and mankind, the man Christ Jesus, who gave Himself as a ransom for all people."

A mediator is someone who stands between two parties to bring them together. In a dispute, a mediator bridges the gap that neither side can cross alone.

And that is precisely our situation. On one side stands a holy God who cannot tolerate sin. On the other side stand sinful people who cannot remove it. The gap between us is infinite — and no amount of effort, religion, or good intention can close it.

But Jesus stands in that gap.

He is uniquely qualified. He is fully God — so He can represent the Father. He is fully man — so He can represent us. And He gave Himself as a ransom — so the debt between us could be settled.

Hebrews 9:15 adds:

"For this reason Christ is the mediator of a new covenant, that those who are called may receive the promised eternal inheritance — now that He has died as a ransom to set them free."

His mediation is not ongoing negotiation. It is accomplished fact. He died. The ransom was paid. The covenant was sealed. And the inheritance is ours.

This table is where we remember the Mediator. Not a distant God we cannot approach. Not a hopeless gap we cannot cross. But a Savior who bridged the divide with His own body and blood.

This bread — the body of the One who stood between us and judgment.
This cup — the blood of the Mediator who brought us to God.

There is one way to the Father. And His name is Jesus.

Let's remember Him now. Let's pray.
